title: CDMGNotation

tex_statement.html:
Let $G=(J,V,E,L)$ be a CDMG. We will write:

  1. $v \in G$ to mean $v \in J \cup V$,
  2. $v_1 \tuh v_2 \in G$ to mean $(v_1,v_2) \in E$,
  3. $v_1 \hut v_2 \in G$ to mean $(v_2,v_1) \in E$,
  4. $v_1 \huh v_2 \in G$ to mean $(v_1,v_2) \in L$,
  5. $v_1 \suh v_2 \in G$ to mean that either $v_1 \tuh v_2 \in G$ or $v_1 \huh v_2 \in G$,
  6. $v_1 \hus v_2 \in G$ to mean that either $v_1 \hut v_2 \in G$ or $v_1 \huh v_2 \in G$,
  7. $v_1 \sus v_2 \in G$ to mean that either $v_1 \tuh v_2 \in G$ or $v_1 \hut v_2 \in G$ or $v_1 \huh v_2 \in G$.

The star stands for a placeholder to mean: ‚Äúarrowhead or tail‚ÄĚ.

lean.title: CDMGNotation -- vertex membership
lean.comments:
--
-- `v ‚ąą G` means `v ‚ąą G.J ‚ą™ G.V`, i.e. `v` is either an input node or
-- an output node of the CDMG `G`. We register this as a `Membership`
-- instance so the literal Lean syntax `v ‚ąą G` typechecks and unfolds
-- to set-union membership.
/-
Verbatim from `lecture-notes/lecture_notes/graphs.tex` (def 3.2,
item 1, in `\begin{Not}\label{not-cdmg}`):

  $v \in G$ to mean $v \in J \cup V$,
-/
lean.statement:
instance : Membership őĪ (CDMG őĪ) where
  mem G v := v ‚ąą G.J ‚ą™ G.V
@[simp] theorem CDMG.mem_iff {G : CDMG őĪ} {v : őĪ} :
    v ‚ąą G ‚ÜĒ v ‚ąą G.J ‚ą™ G.V := Iff.rfl
namespace CDMG

lean.title: CDMGNotation -- directed, reverse-directed, bidirected edges
lean.comments:
--
-- `tuh` / `hut` / `huh` mirror the LN macros `\tuh` / `\hut` / `\huh`.
-- They are `Prop`-valued one-line aliases for membership in `G.E`
-- (directed) or `G.L` (bidirected). The names are deliberately kept
-- close to the LN macros so a reader bouncing between the notes and
-- the Lean source can recognise them.
/-
Verbatim from `lecture-notes/lecture_notes/graphs.tex` (def 3.2,
items 2-4):

  $v_1 \tuh v_2 \in G$ to mean $(v_1,v_2) \in E$,
  $v_1 \hut v_2 \in G$ to mean $(v_2,v_1) \in E$,
  $v_1 \huh v_2 \in G$ to mean $(v_1,v_2) \in L$,
-/

/-- LN's `\tuh`: there is a *directed* edge `v‚āĀ ‚Üí v‚āā` in `G`, i.e.
`(v‚āĀ, v‚āā) ‚ąą G.E`. -/
lean.statement:
def tuh (G : CDMG őĪ) (v‚āĀ v‚āā : őĪ) : Prop := (v‚āĀ, v‚āā) ‚ąą G.E
def hut (G : CDMG őĪ) (v‚āĀ v‚āā : őĪ) : Prop := (v‚āā, v‚āĀ) ‚ąą G.E
def huh (G : CDMG őĪ) (v‚āĀ v‚āā : őĪ) : Prop := (v‚āĀ, v‚āā) ‚ąą G.L

lean.title: CDMGNotation -- arrowhead-at-target / -source / any edge
lean.comments:
--
-- The "star" in the LN means "arrowhead or tail"; these three
-- relations are exactly the disjunctions of the primitive edges.
/-
Verbatim from `lecture-notes/lecture_notes/graphs.tex` (def 3.2,
items 5-7):

  $v_1 \suh v_2 \in G$ to mean that either $v_1 \tuh v_2 \in G$
                                       or $v_1 \huh v_2 \in G$,
  $v_1 \hus v_2 \in G$ to mean that either $v_1 \hut v_2 \in G$
                                       or $v_1 \huh v_2 \in G$,
  $v_1 \sus v_2 \in G$ to mean that either $v_1 \tuh v_2 \in G$
                                       or $v_1 \hut v_2 \in G$
                                       or $v_1 \huh v_2 \in G$.

The star stands for a placeholder to mean: "arrowhead or tail".
-/

/-- LN's `\suh`: there is an edge with an arrowhead at `v‚āā` in `G`,
i.e. either `tuh G v‚āĀ v‚āā` (`‚Üí`) or `huh G v‚āĀ v‚āā` (`‚ÜĒ`). -/
lean.statement:
def suh (G : CDMG őĪ) (v‚āĀ v‚āā : őĪ) : Prop := tuh G v‚āĀ v‚āā ‚ą® huh G v‚āĀ v‚āā
def hus (G : CDMG őĪ) (v‚āĀ v‚āā : őĪ) : Prop := hut G v‚āĀ v‚āā ‚ą® huh G v‚āĀ v‚āā
def sus (G : CDMG őĪ) (v‚āĀ v‚āā : őĪ) : Prop :=
  tuh G v‚āĀ v‚āā ‚ą® hut G v‚āĀ v‚āā ‚ą® huh G v‚āĀ v‚āā
scoped notation:50 v‚āĀ " ‚ü∂[" G "] " v‚āā => Causality.CDMG.tuh G v‚āĀ v‚āā
scoped notation:50 v‚āĀ " ‚üĶ[" G "] " v‚āā => Causality.CDMG.hut G v‚āĀ v‚āā
scoped notation:50 v‚āĀ " ‚ü∑[" G "] " v‚āā => Causality.CDMG.huh G v‚āĀ v‚āā
scoped notation:50 v‚āĀ " ‚áł[" G "] " v‚āā => Causality.CDMG.suh G v‚āĀ v‚āā
scoped notation:50 v‚āĀ " ‚á∑[" G "] " v‚āā => Causality.CDMG.hus G v‚āĀ v‚āā
scoped notation:50 v‚āĀ " ‚Üģ[" G "] " v‚āā => Causality.CDMG.sus G v‚āĀ v‚āā
end CDMG
end Causality

lean_explanation:
This file equips the `CDMG` structure (from def 3.1) with the seven notations from the lecture notes' `\begin{Not}` block.

Vertex membership is registered as a `Membership` instance, so the literal Lean syntax `v ‚ąą G` typechecks and unfolds to `v ‚ąą G.J ‚ą™ G.V`. A `@[simp]` lemma `CDMG.mem_iff` exposes this defining equation, after which Mathlib's `Set.mem_union`, `Set.mem_insert_iff`, etc. all become available automatically.

The three primitive edge relations `tuh`, `hut`, `huh` are `Prop`-valued one-line aliases for membership in `G.E` (directed) or `G.L` (bidirected). Their names deliberately mirror the LaTeX macros `\tuh`, `\hut`, `\huh` so readers can move between notes and Lean source easily. The three "star" relations `suh`, `hus`, `sus` are defined as disjunctions of the primitives, exactly matching the lecture notes' phrasing.

Each relation is paired with a `scoped notation` declaration under `Causality.CDMG`. The chosen Unicode arrows (`‚ü∂[G]`, `‚üĶ[G]`, `‚ü∑[G]`, `‚áł[G]`, `‚á∑[G]`, `‚Üģ[G]`) echo the LaTeX macros while staying clear of Lean's built-in `‚Üí`/`‚Üź`/`‚ÜĒ`. The `[G]` suffix carries the graph argument explicitly at the notation level. Users opt in with `open scoped Causality.CDMG`.

Key Mathlib idioms on display: the `Membership` typeclass pattern (so `‚ąą` interacts cleanly with `simp`), `scoped notation` to keep Unicode tokens namespace-local, and `Prop`-valued set membership via `G.E : Set (őĪ √ó őĪ)`.
design_choices:
Several deliberate trade-offs are documented:

**`Membership` instance vs. a bespoke `CDMG.mem` predicate.** The notes write `v ‚ąą G`, so a `Membership` instance gives us the lecture-notes syntax verbatim and lets `v ‚ąą G` rewrite via `CDMG.mem_iff` into Mathlib's full `Set` API. A separate predicate would force `CDMG.mem G v` everywhere and would not interact with `mem_*` lemmas.

**Six separate `def`s vs. one inductive `EdgeKind` enum.** A `EdgeKind`-tagged `hasEdge` predicate was rejected because (1) the notes treat each relation as an *atom* in proofs (claim 3.1, def 3.5 parents-set, etc.), and atoms compose better with `simp`, `rw`, and pattern matching; and (2) the three star relations are genuine disjunctions, not single edge types, so an enum would not naturally represent them.

**Unicode arrows with `[G]` suffix.** Long arrows `‚ü∂`/`‚üĶ`/`‚ü∑` are used instead of the heavily overloaded `‚Üí`/`‚Üź`/`‚ÜĒ`. The stroked arrows `‚áł`/`‚á∑`/`‚Üģ` visually evoke the lecture-notes "star = arrowhead-or-tail" placeholder. The `[G]` suffix avoids overloading Lean's `‚ąą` while keeping the graph argument explicit.

**`Prop`-valued, not `Bool`.** This matches the notes and avoids forcing `DecidableEq őĪ` -- later chapters use uncountable (e.g. real-valued) vertex types, so decidability cannot be assumed at this layer.

**`scoped` notation.** Keeping the arrows under `Causality.CDMG` means files that don't `open scoped Causality.CDMG` are not burdened with the Unicode tokens.
